content.text: Splitting a GenAI application into microservices solves scalability and deployment isolation — but it creates a new problem: which team is responsible for which artefact, and who must review a change when that artefact crosses service boundaries? Without clear ownership boundaries, a prompt engineer modifies a tool schema they do not fully understand, a tools team accidentally weakens an eval dataset, or a platform team changes an agent config without notifying the safety team.

This sub-subtopic maps the principal ownership boundaries that arise in GenAI microservice architectures and provides the CODEOWNERS patterns, interface contracts, and shared-directory conventions to enforce them.

attrs.alt: Ownership boundary map across GenAI microservices

content.text: Most GenAI applications, regardless of how many services they contain, reduce to three ownership concerns:
Behaviour regression, tone drift, jailbreak surface expansion
Tool schemas, API adapters, auth tokens
Capability creep, injection vectors, over-privileged functions
Silent quality regression, undetected prompt degradation
Each domain should be a separate CODEOWNERS block. Cross-domain artefacts (shared contracts, API specifications) need sign-off from all affected teams.
(one repo per service), the CODEOWNERS file is simple — every file belongs to the service team. For a
the pattern must encode service boundaries explicitly:

# .github/CODEOWNERS — GenAI monorepo
# Service teams own their own directories.
# shared/ requires sign-off from both publisher and consumers.

# --- Prompt Service ---
services/prompt-service/                @prompt-team @nlp-lead
services/prompt-service/prompts/        @prompt-team @nlp-lead @ai-safety-team
services/prompt-service/templates/      @prompt-team @nlp-lead

# --- Tool Gateway Service ---
services/tool-gateway/                  @tools-team @security-review
services/tool-gateway/schemas/          @tools-team @security-review @architecture-lead
services/tool-gateway/auth/             @tools-team @security-review @infra-team

# --- Eval Service ---
services/eval-service/                  @ai-safety-team @ml-eng
services/eval-service/datasets/         @ai-safety-team @ml-eng @nlp-lead
services/eval-service/benchmarks/       @ai-safety-team @ml-eng

# --- Agent Orchestration Service ---
services/agent-service/                 @ai-platform-team @architecture-lead
services/agent-service/configs/         @ai-platform-team @architecture-lead @nlp-lead

# --- Shared contracts (owner + all consumers must approve changes) ---
shared/tool-schemas/                    @tools-team @prompt-team @ai-platform-team
shared/eval-criteria/                   @ai-safety-team @prompt-team @ml-eng
shared/agent-interfaces/               @ai-platform-team @tools-team @prompt-team

# --- Infrastructure (all services depend on this) ---
infra/                                  @devops @architecture-lead
infra/k8s/                             @devops @architecture-lead @ai-platform-team

# --- CI/CD pipeline definitions ---
.github/                               @ai-platform-team @devops
.github/CODEOWNERS                     @ai-platform-team @architecture-lead
The hardest ownership problem in GenAI microservices is the

: a tool schema that the prompt service reads to format its tool call instructions, and the tool gateway reads to validate incoming requests. Both teams depend on it, but only one team should own (write) it.
The convention that works in practice:
— the team that generates the schema (Tool Gateway) is the authoritative writer. They own the path under
and maintain a build step that copies the canonical version to
All consumers are co-owners in CODEOWNERS
requires a review from every team listed: publisher, prompt team, agent team. This prevents unilateral changes to a shared contract.
of the canonical source; changes flow from the owning service's directory through CI, never edited in place.

# .github/workflows/sync-shared-contracts.yml
# Runs after any merge to services/tool-gateway/schemas/
# Copies the canonical tool schema to shared/tool-schemas/
# and opens a PR for consumer team review.

name: Sync Shared Tool Schemas

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "services/tool-gateway/schemas/**"

jobs:
  sync:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Copy schemas to shared/
        run: |
          rsync -av --delete \
            services/tool-gateway/schemas/ \
            shared/tool-schemas/

      - name: Open PR for consumer review
        uses: peter-evans/create-pull-request@v6
        with:
          title: "sync: update shared tool schemas from tool-gateway"
          body: |
            Auto-generated from changes to services/tool-gateway/schemas/.
            
            **Required reviewers (CODEOWNERS):**
            - @tools-team (publisher) 
            - @prompt-team (consumer: prompt formatting)
            - @ai-platform-team (consumer: agent orchestration)
          branch: "sync/tool-schemas-${{ github.sha }}"
          commit-message: "sync(shared): update tool schemas"

A pull request that modifies files in two different service directories is a red flag — it suggests the PR is either doing too much (should be split) or it is touching a shared contract without going through the shared/ sync process. Detect this automatically:

— the OpenAPI spec or Protobuf definition — is a special shared artefact: changes to it break consumers. For GenAI microservices, treat every API contract as a shared/ artefact with consumer co-ownership:

# API specs: publisher owns the definition; all consumers are co-reviewers
api-specs/prompt-service.openapi.yaml     @prompt-team @ai-platform-team
api-specs/tool-gateway.openapi.yaml       @tools-team @prompt-team @ai-platform-team
api-specs/eval-service.openapi.yaml       @ai-safety-team @ai-platform-team

# Protobuf / gRPC definitions follow the same pattern
proto/prompt_service.proto                @prompt-team @ai-platform-team
proto/tool_gateway.proto                  @tools-team @prompt-team @ai-platform-team

When a consumer team reviews an API contract change they are signing off that the change does not break their integration — not that the change is correct from the publisher's perspective. Both reviews are necessary.

Ownership boundaries define who reviews code changes during the build phase. They should map directly to the on-call rotation during the production phase — the team that owns the prompt service should also be the one paged when the prompt service causes a production incident.
When a single model behaviour incident touches multiple services, the escalation path should converge on a
where all owning teams coordinate. Pre-configure this in your incident management tool so that any paged team for an AI incident can immediately pull in the others.
The ownership boundary problem manifests differently depending on whether the GenAI system lives in one repository or many.
Entire repo belongs to one team — CODEOWNERS is optional but still useful for critical paths
CODEOWNERS is mandatory; without it, any developer can merge into any service
Published as versioned packages (PyPI, npm); breaking changes require semver bump
Shared/ directory with co-ownership in CODEOWNERS
Impossible by default — each service is a separate repo and PR
Possible and dangerous; boundary violation checks in CI are required
Create new repo, assign team — ownership is clear from creation
Must add CODEOWNERS entries; forgetting means the service has no ownership
Each repo has its own PR history
Shared history; easier to trace cross-service dependencies
Neither model is universally better. The key is that ownership is
— not assumed. In a polyrepo, document which team owns which repo in a central service catalog; in a monorepo, the CODEOWNERS file is that catalog.
For larger organisations with many GenAI microservices, maintaining ownership exclusively in CODEOWNERS becomes unwieldy. A

— a machine-readable manifest listing every service, its owning team, its dependencies, and its on-call rotation — complements CODEOWNERS and enables tooling like automated docs, dependency graphs, and blast-radius analysis.

# service-catalog.yaml
# Machine-readable manifest for all GenAI microservices.
# Used by tooling for ownership queries, blast-radius analysis, and on-call routing.

services:
  - id: prompt-service
    name: Prompt Service
    description: Versioned prompt registry and template rendering for all agents
    team: "@prompt-team"
    tech_lead: "@nlp-lead"
    safety_reviewer: "@ai-safety-team"
    repo_path: services/prompt-service/
    codeowners_paths:
      - "services/prompt-service/"
      - "shared/tool-schemas/  # consumer"
    api_spec: api-specs/prompt-service.openapi.yaml
    consumes:
      - shared/tool-schemas     # to format tool call instructions
      - shared/eval-criteria    # to validate prompt outputs
    publishes: []
    oncall_rotation: prompt-oncall
    slo_targets:
      latency_p99_ms: 300
      availability: 99.9

  - id: tool-gateway
    name: Tool Gateway Service
    description: Unified entry point for all external tool calls; enforces auth and schemas
    team: "@tools-team"
    tech_lead: "@architecture-lead"
    safety_reviewer: "@security-review"
    repo_path: services/tool-gateway/
    codeowners_paths:
      - "services/tool-gateway/"
      - "shared/tool-schemas/  # publisher"
    api_spec: api-specs/tool-gateway.openapi.yaml
    consumes: []
    publishes:
      - shared/tool-schemas
    oncall_rotation: tools-oncall
    slo_targets:
      latency_p99_ms: 500
      availability: 99.95

  - id: eval-service
    name: Eval Service
    description: Automated eval runner for regression gates in CI and scheduled benchmarks
    team: "@ai-safety-team"
    tech_lead: "@ml-eng"
    safety_reviewer: "@ai-safety-team"
    repo_path: services/eval-service/
    codeowners_paths:
      - "services/eval-service/"
      - "shared/eval-criteria/  # publisher"
    api_spec: api-specs/eval-service.openapi.yaml
    consumes: []
    publishes:
      - shared/eval-criteria
    oncall_rotation: eval-oncall
    slo_targets:
      run_duration_p95_min: 10
      availability: 99.5
With this catalog in place, a script can generate a
co-ownership entry automatically and keep it in sync with
— preventing the catalog and CODEOWNERS from drifting apart.
Blast Radius Analysis Before a Boundary-Crossing Change

Before making a change to a shared contract (tool schema, eval criteria, API spec), quantify the blast radius: which services consume this artefact, which teams must be notified, and which tests must pass before the change can be merged.
Run this script before opening a PR that touches

to know in advance which teams need to be added as reviewers and which CI gates will be required. It can also be embedded in the PR description template as a checklist auto-generated by a GitHub Actions step.

Ownership boundaries in GenAI microservices must be explicit and enforced — the cost of a boundary violation is not a compile error but a production AI behaviour regression that may take days to diagnose. The three foundational patterns are: service-scoped CODEOWNERS blocks, a
convention for shared contracts, and a boundary violation check in CI that flags PRs crossing service domains.

These patterns compound with branch protection and on-call alignment: the team that reviews code changes is the same team paged when those changes cause incidents, creating a closed feedback loop where ownership means accountability.
